summary:
The predominant type of hemoglobin found in adult humans is HbA at around 95-98%. This makes up most of the circulatory system’s oxygen-carrying capacity with minor contributions from other types including a very low amount present due to genetic conditions or diseases if any exist. Agent1 reasoned based primarily on the high prevalence rate of HbA among all possible variants discussed which aligns closely with standard medical knowledge about normal hematological profiles without specific pathological states influencing results significantly here. 

The summary focuses mainly on highlighting how common HbA truly is compared to its counterparts while touching lightly upon why it remains so dominant across populations generally speaking rather than delving deeply into molecular biology aspects related directly towards answering initial query posed regarding what kind exactly does "human" refer too clinically when discussing their blood agent1 (Round 1 summarized): The predominant type of hemoglobin found in adult humans is HbA at around 95-98%. This makes up most of the circulatory system’s oxygen-carrying capacity with minor contributions from other types including a very low amount present due to genetic conditions or diseases if any exist. Agent1 reasoned based primarily on the high prevalence rate of HbA among all possible variants discussed which aligns closely with standard medical knowledge about normal hematological profiles without specific pathological states influencing results significantly here.
